title: Ray Monk - Ludwig Wittgenstein in Vienna 1925-1929
text:
[applause]
Thank you. And um is this coming
through? Okay, good. Um and thanks to
Rad Miller for that very generous
introduction. And thank you also to the
Vickenstein Initiative for inviting me
to this very splendid occasion in this
absolutely wonderful uh uh building with
its uh evocations of the Vickenstein
family. I'm delighted to be here.
So my theme today is Vickenstein
during the years 26 to 29.
Um and I'll center my remarks around
Dorian
this theme.
Vickenstein once said that work on
philosophy is really more work on
oneself.
And what I want to show today is how
that thought guided everything
Bickenstein did and how in particular it
guided what he did during these years 26
to 29. Um which were the forerunner of
his return to philosophy in 29. And I
want to show how in returning to
philosophy
um he was carrying on this um
work on himself that he persisted with
throughout his life. So I will be going
backwards and forwards a bit um to show
how this ideal of Vickenstein's showed
itself from the very beginning and how
it shaped what he did during the years
when he was building the Kmanasa for his
sister Margaret and finally how it finds
expression in his philosophical
thinking.
Okay. So in 26, 26 was the year he
finally gave up being a school teacher
and his reason for becoming a school
teacher in the first place was work on
himself. Um he had arrived back from the
first world war a changed man and I'll
say more about that in a bit. But
one change was he'd abandoned
philosophy. He'd uh the tract datus was
about to be published
and he announced to the dismay of his
family members that what he wanted to do
was train as a school teacher and teach
village children in poor parts of
Austria.
And his sister Hemina said, "Well, look,
this is crazy. This is like using a
precision instrument to open a crate."
And Vickenstein said, "You remind me of
somebody who's looking out of a window
and watching somebody struggle with an
umbrella and bend forth and they cannot
explain this man's movements because
they don't know what storms are
besetting this man." And the analogy
clearly was that he had to he felt that
he had to go into elementary school
teaching
um because of the state of himself. He
thought that this would improve himself.
Um as it turned out he uh his spell
during a teacher was fairly disastrous
although it had its moments and he had a
beneficial impact on a few school
students. um he found it difficult to
assimilate into the life of these
villages in lower Austria and he went to
three. The first one at the top is
Trenbach and then we have Pulkberg and
then we have Otal. Um he stayed a mere
uh two years, not even that uh at each
place. And at each place he ran into
trouble with the his his colleagues and
with the parents of the children that he
taught. And finally at Otal he had to
leave the entire profession.
And so in the summer of 26 he went he
returned to Vienna.
Um
not immediately to as it were the bosom
of his family but to an occupation that
he thought might provide the salvation
his soul needed. Having um the moral
failure as he saw it of his school
teaching career. He went to a monastery,
the hospitalers, the brothers
hospitalers at Hudelledorf
and he worked there as a gardener.
He'd previously done that um at another
monastery in Closter Noberg. Cluster
Noyberg um
which he'd done immediately before his
teacher training in 1919.
um uh and uh before his teaching and the
abbot of this particular monastery had
seen him at work, knew his background uh
as a philosopher and as a highly
educated man and the abbot said, "Oh, I
see that intelligence pays for, you
know, uh counts for something in
gardening, too." Um,
however, Vickens left the monastery uh
after a month or so and returned to
Vienna
to be really welcomed
by his sister Margaret. Um, his mother
Leopoldine Pauly had died that summer.
Um,
and Margaret wasn't the head of the
family. just go back and then we'll go
back to uh Gretle. So go forward there.
Um here's the family and you see in the
middle we've got Carl the father and
Pauli the mother. Uh Carl had died some
years earlier. Pauli died in the summer
of 26 which left as the head of the
family actually herina who is in the
middle of this uh group of five. on the
left. Um we have Helena and then Gretle
is is on the right and then we have Paul
and Ludvig. Um some members of the
family are missing from that photograph
and there's a photograph here of all the
siblings. Uh Helena, Rudolph, Hermina,
Ludvig, Gretle, Paul, Hance, and Kurt.
Um, Hans, Rudy, and Kurt
um, all committed suicide. Uh, Hans and
Rudolph when Ludvig was quite little and
then Kurt during the First World War.
So those three were missing from the
family by the time he had returned after
the after the war and in particular
after in in 1926.
And so the head of the family by that
time was Hermina. But the one with the
greatest influence over Vickenstein was
Gretle. Next one. Uh so now go there. Um
now Gretle had always been the sibling
with the greatest influence over Ludvig.
Um particularly intellectually and
culturally. And one has to bear in mind
that the Vickenstein family occupied a
very central place in the culture of
Vienna which before the first world war
was going through a very exciting
period. New movements were happening uh
in intellectual life and in artistic
life of all types. Freud was developing
psychoanalysis. The Jugenill movement in
art was happening. uh Carl Krauss was uh
writing. In music, you had Johannes
Brahms and the Vickenstein family um in
particular um uh patronized Yoseph
Labour um and the Vickenstein home, the
Pala Vickenstein, which no longer
exists, but the Pala Vickenstein became
a center for uh culture of Vienna,
particularly musical culture. Uh Brahms
was a regular visitor. Um uh Yosef Yokim
uh was known to the Vickenstein family
who also supported the visual arts. Uh
Carl Vickenstein paid for the building
of the secession building um which
exhibited the work of the Yugenill
painters Climpmp and Sheila and Kauoska.
And Climpmpt of course now go back to
and here now Climpmpt of course um
painted a number of famous portraits as
well as his landscapes and so on. And
Carl commissioned Climpmp to paint this
wonderful portrait of uh Gretle which
now hangs in an art museum in in Munich.
Um, but was created uh and paid for uh
created by Climpmpt, paid for by by Carl
because Carl knew the Jugen Still
painters personally and they all had
some connection with him and some reason
for um being grateful to him. Now go
another one there. Now I said that
Gretle influenced Ludvig intellectually.
There are a number of important respects
to this. One that I'd like to pick out
is that it was through Gretle that
Vickenstein became influenced in uh
influenced by the work of Otto Vininger
who was probably the first philosopher
to have uh a deep impact on Vickenstein.
Now, Vinegar's book, Sex and Character,
has a reputation, a justified
reputation, um, for being slightly
deranged. Um, it's, um, it it develops a
view that is straightforwardly mis
misogynistic uh, and anti-semitic.
At the heart of the view though and I
don't think Vickenstein Ludvig
Vickinstein was influenced by that
misogyny or by that anti-semitism
but I think what Ludvig Vickinstein was
influenced by was what lay at the heart
of that which is Vineer's conception of
genius and the duties that go with it.
The view expressed in sex and character
is that every man women were a hopeless
case uh and Jews were a kind of women
woman. So um they weren't they you know
they they couldn't do this but every man
who was not a Jew um had an obligation a
duty to realize the genius that they
were capable of
um
which had two parts to it rigorous
thinking logic and living well ethics
and vic and vinegar says logic and
ethics are fundamentally the same
they're no more than the duty to one's
self and that I think had a powerful
impact on the way Vickinstein saw
himself and his task as a philosopher
and this idea that philosophy is a kind
of work on oneself
is a restatement of Vininger's uh idea
that logic and ethics are dual parts two
part two sides of the same coin which is
the duty to oneself.
Another philosopher that Gretle
introduced Vickenstein to and became a
huge influence um was Schoppenhower
um his book devel
which is translated in English as the
world as will and representation
um presents the kind of metaphysics
which Vickenstein was to reject
ultimately but what he never rejected
was Shopenhau's view of music. this sort
of exalted view of music. No art affects
man so immediately as music as none
other reveals to us the essence of the
universe so profoundly and so
immediately.
Listening to great fullvoiced and
beautiful music is like bathing the
mind. It drains off all that is impure,
petty and bad and lifts everyone to the
highest spiritual plane to which his
nature can attain. And as he listens to
great music, man perceives clearly what
he is worth, or rather what he might be
worth.
There's very little in Vickenstein's
philosophical writing about music. But
music was, so to speak, written into his
soul.
Um, and you can see this when you look
at Vickenstein's manuscripts. Very often
a musical phrase occurs to him and he
writes it down. um music was so to speak
going on in his in his head all the time
and music was the art that he responded
to at the deepest level uh and which he
had the most exalted view of towards the
end of his life he said to his friend
Morris Drury how can I be understood
when I haven't said a word about the
importance that music has to me and I
think one of the things he got from
Schoenhau and uh um indirectly from
Gretle was the expression, the verbal
expression of that attitude to music.
Right now, back to the summer of 26.
Gretle had asked the architect Paul
Engelman to design a house for him. Now,
Vickenstein had already known Paul
Engelman for 10 years. He met Engelman
in 1916 when he was on leave from uh uh
from the Eastern Front. Vickenstein uh
was in an artillery regiment fighting
the Russians on the Eastern front and he
had some leave and he went to Olmutz in
Moravia and there he met Paul Engelman
who had been discharged from the army
and Vickenstein and Engelman became very
close friends and Engelman
in this quotation
says why in his view they were so close.
He says, "In me, Vickenstein
unexpectedly met a person who, like
members of the younger generation,
suffered acutely under the discrepancy
between the world as it is and as it
ought to be, according to his lights,
but who tended also to seek the source
of that discre discrepancy within rather
than outside himself. That is absolutely
crucial. this view that the world is not
as it should be but in faced with that
the moral task is to change oneself.
Uh Vickenstein once put it to uh a
friend the only thing you can do to
improve the world is to improve
yourself.
And this was a fundamental uh clash of
values with his tutor at Cambridge uh
pre-war tutor Bertrren Russell who took
exactly the opposite point of view. For
Russell the key ethical task was to
improve the world and he didn't spend a
great deal of time thinking about or
striving thinking about being a better
person or striving to be a better
person. For Russell that was secondary.
The important thing was what
consequences he had on the world at uh
at large. For Vickenstein, on the
contrary, the important thing was to, as
he often put it, settle accounts with
oneself, to be a better person.
And in Engleman, he had a sympathetic
ear for that view and also somebody who
shared it. And that was crucially
important to Vickenstein in 1916.
So in 192 in 1926 he's given up his
teaching profession. He's gone back to
Vienna uh and he's found that his sister
Gretle whom he admires and who has
influenced him intellectually and
culturally has hired this man Engelman
to design a house.
Now Engelman was a student of Adolf Laws
and Vickenstein had already met Adolf
Laws um and in fact actually had already
given him money. Uh in 1914 Vickenstein
donated quite a large sum of money to be
distributed to uh artists uh uh
architects and musicians. And the person
he chose to do do that uh to distribute
the money was Ludvig Vanfiker
um who was the editor uh of a journal
based in Insbrook. And through that
donation Adolf L met some money and FA
introduced Vickenstein to Adolf L. And
when the two met
before the first world war they were
hugely impressed with each other.
Vickenstein wrote excitedly to von
Vicker saying it was a delight to meet
Adolf LS and Adolf LS uh is repeated to
have said to Vickenstein you are me.
They their their their minds were in
such a chord with regard to the values
that an architect should express in his
or her work.
At the heart of those values was a
rejection of anything artificial.
And so it the ethical task embraced by
the architect
infused with Adolf Lul's vision of
architecture was to reject anything that
smacked of vanity or falseeness or
anything that lacked integrity. In other
words, exactly the things that
Vickenstein felt should be expuned from
one's personal self.
So Adolf Loss's view of architecture
is mirrored by Vickenstein's view of
what makes a decent human being. One
must strive to eliminate the dishonest,
the unnecessary, the artificial, the
merely decorative.
And likewise, Adolf L uh championed the
view that that is what should happen in
architecture. And so Engelman's design
for Gretle's house, uh, this is what it
ended up looking like, um, having been
strongly, uh, influenced and shaped by
Vickenstein in collaboration with
Engelman. And you can see it's a starkly
unadorned house. Um, not to everybody's
taste, not to everybody in the uh,
Vickenstein family's taste. Hermina, for
example, didn't like it at all. She
described it as house embodied logic.
Um,
uh, Gretle rather liked it. Although
we're in this splendid place. This is
where Gretle chose to live for seven
years. Um the aesthetic in this
wonderful building around us couldn't be
more different from the aesthetic which
governs the design of the K Manasa house
that Engelman and Vickenstein uh
together designed uh for Gretle.
Now when Vickenstein and Engelman met in
1916, as Engelman said, they were
sympathetic uh at a at a deep ethical
and personal uh level.
And one of the things that united them
was their conviction of the importance
of the inexpressable.
Vickenstein
before the first world war had developed
a view of logic and I'll say perhaps a
little bit more about that uh in a
moment but at the heart of his view of
logic was a distinction between what one
can say and what has to be shown
and now he when in the first world war
he carried on working on this book which
became tract's logical philosophus
published in English in 1922
and
that book is as it were 67ths about
logic and 17th about what Vickenstein
called the mystical.
It says there are indeed things that
cannot be put into words. They make
themselves manifest.
Now, Engelman shared that view
and to illustrate it sent to Vickenstein
when Vickenstein was at the front.
Engelman sent in this poem by
Ludviguland
uh Count Abberah's Hawthorne
uh and I'll just read it fairly quickly.
Count abah Russellbeard from
Vertonberg's fair land on holy arant
steered to Palestina's strand the while
he slowly rode along a woodland way he
cut from the haworth bush a little fresh
green spray then in his iron helm the
little sprig he placed and bore it in
the walls and oh the ocean waste and
when he reached his home he placed it in
the earth where little leaves and buds
the gentle spring called forth he went
each year to it the count so brave and
true. And overjoyed was he to witness
how it grew. The count was worn with
age, the sprig became a tree beneath
which the old man oft would sit in
revery. The branching arch so high whose
whispers is so bland reminds him of the
past and of Palestina's strand
beautifully simply written.
Go back one back. Um and in sending it
to Vickenstein, Engelman commented uh
Vickenstein commented to Engelman having
received the poem, "This is how it is.
If only you do not try to utter what is
unutterable, then nothing gets lost. But
the unutterable will be unutterably
contained in what has been uttered."
The poem doesn't, as it were, attempt to
go inside Count Abahad's mind. It
doesn't it doesn't put into words what
when he reflects on the past what what
exactly he's reflecting on what his
thoughts are. It doesn't put those into
words. It merely gives us an image of
Count Abahad sitting under the tree that
has grown uh from the the little uh
seedling [snorts] and reflecting on his
days in Palestine
and bec precisely according to
Vickenstein and Engelman precisely
because Ouland doesn't try to put into
words Count Abahad's thoughts he gives
us unutterably as it were what It is he
allows us to uh feel and to experience
those thoughts themselves.
A view that Vickenstein summarized in
culture and value with the remark which
applies to the house that Engelman and
he designed uh in these years the Kunt
Mangasa. In art it is hard to say
anything that is as good as saying
nothing.
And that's a view that he resolutely
clung to in every aspect of art and
culture and philosophy.
Right now the contrast here with
Engelman uh in Engleman's case is with
Burton Russell
and going right back to Vickenstein's
introduction to philosophy.
What drew him into philosophy was the
philosophy of mathematics of Burton
Russell which he came across when he was
a student of engineering in Manchester.
He went to Manchester in 1908. Um he
worked on aeronautical engineering. He
designed a propeller. He patented a
propeller.
But more and more he got interested in
mathematics
because the design of a propeller is
essentially a mathematical task.
And he went to he started attending
lectures on pure mathematics.
And then he started thinking about pure
mathematics from a philosophical point
of view and asked his friends at uh
Manchester, well has anybody written on
this? They said, "Oh yes, Burton Russell
in 1903 published this book, Principles
of Mathematics. You should read that."
So Vickenstein read it and became
absolutely obsessed with it
because it leaves it leaves the reader
with a problem. The principles of
mathematics is quite an unusual
philosophical book in that it doesn't
put forward a view a philosophical view
with which the author is satisfied. What
Russell does in that is say that
philosophically mathematics should be
seen as nothing more than logic.
But he himself had discovered a
fundamental problem with that which is
known as Russell's paradox
which I love explaining and I'm going to
explain it now. Um,
what I'm about to say might sound like
gibberish and if it does, don't worry.
We'll just forget it never happened and
we'll we'll move on. Uh, but it might be
worth going into Russell's paradox.
Okay, so here it is.
In his efforts to show that mathematics
was really logic, Russell developed a
view that numbers which of course we are
the fundamental objects of mathematics
that numbers are really what he called
classes and classes are sets of things.
Now a class is
defined by what Russell called a
propositional function and a
propositional function is a proposition
with a variable. So Plato is a
philosopher is a proposition. Aristotle
is a philosopher is a proposition. X is
a philosopher is a propositional
function. Now the class of philosophers
is all the things that will satisfy that
propositional function. All the X's that
are philosophers.
And then the number two for example will
be a class and therefore absorbable into
the theory of classes which is logic.
And if Russell can show that he can show
that mathematics is logic. So everything
depends on showing that numbers are
classes.
But now he discovered a problem with the
very notion of class which is now called
Russell's paradox which is this look it
begins with the reflection that you can
form the class of all classes.
That class will have an unusual
property. It will be a member of itself.
So some classes will be in members of
themselves, some won't. And now form the
class of all the classes that are not
members of themselves.
And here your head might be spinning.
Don't worry about it. The class of all
the classes that are not members of
themselves. And now ask about that
class. Is it a member of itself or not?
And the answer is if yes, then no. And
if no, then yes. In other words, you
have an inescapable contradiction, a
paradox. And that's no good in logic.
You can't build logic on contradiction.
And so the whole thing grinds to a halt.
And Russell published principles of
mathematics in 1903 saying, "Here's a
wonderful theory of mathematics. It's
really logic. Only here's a problem, and
I haven't got a good solution to this
problem."
Vickenstein read it and became really
excited that here was a chance for him
to do what Viner had told him he ought
to do, which is to develop the genius of
which he was capable. And Vickenstein
thought, here's here's an opportunity
for me to develop a work of genius. I
have to solve this problem.
And so in in in in 1911, he left his
engineering studies in Manchester, got a
train down to Cambridge. He hadn't
bothered, I mean, being Vickinstein,
being a member of the Vickingstein
family with all the uh grandeur that
goes with that, he didn't bother doing
anything like signing up as a student
for uh Cambridge University. He just got
a train down to Cambridge, appeared at
Russell's lectures, sat down, and
started arguing with Russell.
Russell didn't say, "Who the hell are
you?"
um he engaged in philosophical
discussion with Vickinstein and within a
few months became convinced that
Vickenstein was a genius and was indeed
the future of philosophy
and so Russell looked to Vickenstein to
solve this problem.
Now Vickenstein solved it with what I
mentioned earlier the distinction
between saying and showing.
Vickenstein's view was that Russell had
got into problems by trying to say
something that can't be said
and that really if you
if you keep within the limits of
language and only try to say that which
can be said, you won't run into these
problems.
For example, with regard to the
difference between an individual thing
and a class, you don't need a theory to
state that you just need different
symbols. Typically in in in in logic, we
have symbols like little x, y, and zed
for things and symbols like capital uh p
and q for properties of things.
So Vickenstein said all you need is
different symbols for different kinds of
things and you don't need a theory. In
fact, a theory that tries to say why
you've got these rules will defeat
itself, will inevitably result in
contradiction. So this distinction
between saying and showing was
Vickenstein's way of solving this
problem thereby obeying Vininger's
instruction to as it were do justice to
whatever work of genius he was capable
of.
And so he he thought that he had nothing
more to learn from Russell and announced
to Russell that what he was going to do
was go as far north as he could to the
north of Norway who's going to build a
house there and he's going to work on
his own on his theory of logic.
And Russell in a letter uh at this time
said um that Vickenstein explained this
alarming plan. And Russell said, "I said
it would be dark." and he said he hated
daylight. I said it would be lonely and
he said he prostituted his mind talking
to intelligent people. I said he was mad
and he said God preserve him from
sanity. God certainly will.
So Vickenstein left Cambridge went to
Norway.
In Norway
he developed this idea of saying and
showing.
And
remember he's he hasn't published hardly
anything at this point. published one
little review, that's all. But already
people are convinced of his genius. So
much so that GE Moore, who was generally
regarded as the most important British
philosopher of his time, was prepared to
go all the way to Sheldon in northern
Norway to act as Vickenstein's secretary
and Vickenstein dictated notes to Gmore
explaining this idea of saying and
showing. Um, logical so-called
propositions show the logical properties
of language but say nothing. every real
proposition shows something besides what
it says about the universe.
Now,
as well as these reflections on logic
and mathematics,
Vickenstein was constantly surprising
Russell with remarks of a broadly
religious spiritual nature.
We know this because Russell wrote about
three letters a day to his lover Otelene
moral in which this ongoing conversation
with Vickenstein is recorded including
this interesting moment when Vickenstein
surprised Russell. Russell hadn't
Russell thought that Vickenstein was
vehemently anti- relligion. But
Vickenstein surprised Russell by quoting
from the Bible from Mark from the Gospel
according to Mark uh 8:36. What shall it
profit a man if he gain the whole world
and lose his soul? And Russell said he
then went on to say how few people there
are who don't lose their soul.
Now when Russ when Vickenstein was in
Norway he wrote on a regular basis to
Russell explaining the development of
his theory of logic and also explaining
so to speak the work on himself.
Deep inside me there's a perpetual
seething like the bottom of a giza and I
keep hoping that things will come to an
eruption once and for all so that I can
turn into a different person. That for
Vickenstein was the aim to turn into a
different person.
Perhaps you regard this thinking about
myself as a waste of time. But how can I
be a legician before I'm a human being?
And then it's very sad but I've once
again no logical news for you. The
reason is that things have gone terribly
badly for me in the last few weeks.
Every day I was tormented by a frightful
angst. It's terrifying beyond
description the kinds of torment, mental
torment that there can be. So alongside
his logic, he's thinking about himself
and he regards these two things as
Vineer had articulated in Sex and
Character as two sides of the same
thing.
And again, he surprised Russell with his
remarks about William James's book, The
Varieties of Religious Experience. This
book does me a lot of good, he says. I
don't mean to say that I will be a
saint, but I'm sure that it does not uh
but I'm not sure that it does not
improve me a little in a way in which
I'd very much like to improve.
Um
now one of the things that James says in
this book he looks at people who've
converted um to a religious point of
view and one of the things he notices is
the importance of facing death. William
James writes, "No matter what a person's
frailties otherwise may be, if he be
willing to risk death, still more if he
suffer it heroically in the service he
has chosen, the fact consecrates him
forever. If he be willing to risk
death."
Now I said that Vickenstein returned
from the first world war a different
person. This was exactly why he went
into the war in order to be a different
person.
in order to face death and then undergo
the experiences that William James is
describing in varieties of religious
experience. And he kept a diary during
the war. And in his diary in September
14, the first time he came face tof face
with the Russian army, he writes, "Now I
have the chance to be a decent human
being, for I'm standing eye to eye with
death." That for him was the whole point
of going into the war to come face to
face with death and undergo this
experience that William James describes
as [clears throat]
central to religious experience.
Now while he was in serving in in in the
war in the first world war he went into
a bookshop in Galia
and the only book he could find was the
gospel in brief by Leo Toltoy. So he
bought it and he read it and he read it
and he read it again and he knew it by
heart.
And
when he went to the Russian front in
1916
his writing changed.
Prior to that Vickenstein had written
two sets of remarks. philosophical
remarks in normal German and personal
remarks in a code, a code that he'd
learned from Gretle um that the
Vickenstein children used to use to, you
know, hide what they'd written uh from
prying eyes, so to speak. And um
in 1916,
he suddenly writes this, "What do I know
about God and the purpose of life?" But
the point is that this is written not in
code but in ordinary language.
It's as if now
in thinking about what he knows about
God the personal and the philosophical
have come together
influenced of course by Toltoy's uh
gospel in brief. So what do I know about
God and the purpose of life? To believe
in God means to understand the meaning
of life. To believe in God means to see
that the facts of the world are not the
end of the matter. The solution to the
problem of life is to be seen in the
disappearance of the problem. Isn't this
the reason why men to whom the meaning
of life has become clear after long
doubting could not say what the meaning
consists in? So now the inexpressable
